sentences.text: I met friend, and then I ask him where is-- because when I left, there was no ghetto.
When I left, there was no ghetto.
Later, months later, they made the ghetto.
So everybody has to go from their houses in one place and they made a ghetto.
Now, the ghetto, they put in two, three families in one place.
That's why a lot of people got sick.
There was no cleanliness.
There's no showers to take.
There's nothing.
You have to live like-- like an animal.
And there's no food.
So a lot of people got typhus.
And a lot of people died in the ghetto.
So when I came to the ghetto, I ask him, where's Ruthie?
He say, Ruthie is there.
Your brother's there.
Ruthie's brother in-law is there.
And there's about 15 to 20 people at this apartment.
So when I came, all right.
I was happy, you know.
I couldn't tell.
You know, she-- we came back.
And my brother's there, and the whole.
So anyway, I was there.
This was in December.
And-- What did you-- what did you say to each other when you saw--
What did we say?
You know, happy we there, but not so-- not so happy for the future.
We didn't see no future.
Because it started getting worse and worse.
With food, with everything.
So--
She was any different.
Oh, sure.
Skinny, you know, and that stuff.
Morris, the baby.
You know, a lot of problems.
But before that, what I was talking, this is before going away to the-- to the concentration camp, and I went to [POLISH] for food.
Because there was no food in the city.

So I took that foreman, Lega, and I put up all the food, went the next day-- put up all the food and I went to see another man, Stefan Marko, what he had our-- he was taken care on our land, what we had the three acres.
I went to see him.
He gave me food.
And my-- also my family, my grandma was still there.
And my uncles, I got a whole wagon with food.
And this was a lot of food.
A full wagon, you know.
Like a wagon with food, with-- you know, all kind of-- all kind of bread and all kind of stuff, you know, and barley--
Everything filled up on the wagon.
And we came back to Jaworow, because we came back there was-- there was the German police standing on the road.
And he was very afraid that they check him.
So I say, cover everything up.
And I made myself, you know, like sick, laying with the handkerchief, putting away.
I said, this way they wouldn't bother you.
You say you taking me to the doctor, to the city.
So when they look, he say, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
What is that?
He say, this is my son.
This is my son.
He is sick.
I'm taking him to the doctor.
That's the way we smuggled in.
He don't check.
When he would check with so much food going in.
We went-- right away would have been bad.
Now, when we came in already, like two blocks away where we were and where-- where Ruthie lived, and this was not a ghetto yet.
So going to the main street, we had to pass by the main street about-- the police.
There was gestapo.
There was police in uniforms going back and forth, and to go through.
So he say, in Ukrainian, Leon.
I'm not going to go there.
I am afraid they're going to catch me and you.
I can't go.
And there's no other way to come there.
You have to pass that road.
So I say, you know what.
You stay here.
I'll let you off.
You stay here.
I'll take the horse and wagon.
I'll take by myself.
And I'll go to the place not where my wife was living, to our cousin's.
Our cousin with her-- with her husband, he used to have a bakery.
He was baking pretzels before the war.
He had a stove.
And over there, I'll put all the food there.
And when we give to family, to friends, you know, to some people what they need it, to give them the food, from there he's going to bake the bread.
So I say, I'm going to take the horse and wagon and you, I'm going to meet by the church.
At the church before farmers used to come out, over there, and selling stuff.
There was a big place.
I say, I'll meet you there with the horse and wagon.
OK.
He was released.
He was so happy.
And then I went with the horse and wagon.
And I see, wait till I have a chance.
One goes this way and one goes this way.
So I have a chance.
And I gave a bit of horse, you know, and with the [POLISH] one way and the other, my horses went off.
And we went through.
It was about three, four blocks away.
And then I stopped.
I called them up.
They know-- they didn't know, but they were all-- a lot of them were there because he was baking sometimes bread.
So a lot of people there, they came and jumped on the wagon, start taking off all the food and everything.
Later, I brought him the wagon and horses.
I brought them back and he went.
And he went.
But we had for a while, we had food.
So this was the thing.
But I want to stop and tell you about when I arrive into the village and get some food.
Now, later in the ghetto, when it was the ghetto already, and my brother was there, and my cousin San from Krakow was there.
My brother David was there, and my brother Yankel, he was in the-- in Russia.
He was in Russia.
So when we were there, and we were also dealing with matches.
People come in.
They sell you matches.
They sell you cigarettes or [POLISH].
So my brother was dealing, going in the ghetto, you know, to make a couple zlotys or whatever, you know.
So it came to about February.
I think in February, 1993, there was another liquidation.
February or-- I think in February was another liquidation of the ghetto.
They were-- they were grabbing people, grabbing people to-- to hard labor camps.
An action.
An action, yes.
There was another action.
But we hide in our cousin's bunker, in another cousin's bunker.
And before that, my wife was hiding in a bunker by a friend.
And the gestapo made an action.
She was hiding in a bunker.
What this friend told her, here, listen.
If you don't have where to run and there's an action, you come and I'll show you where my bunker is.
She had a bunker under the bed.
Move away the bed, you pick up a board.
You pick up a board and you crawl in in the bunker.
Now, later there was a little carpet that you put on top of the board.
You crawl in so the carpet stays.
A little carpet.
Just to close it up.
What's happened, there was an action.
And she had-- Morris was a little-- little baby.
So-- and she heard the something-- what-- how does an action work?
An action where they give three shots in the air in every corner.
First they go around on all the corners.
They go around.
And they give three shots in each corner.
So we know already how the action looks.
So when she heard the first three shots, she ran right away out and took the baby.
She had also a girl with her from another city where she was sleeping over there.
And she say-- Rachel was her name.
She say, Rachel.
Come on right away.
Don't dress up.
Come on with me in the bunker.
And luckily, not a day before, she told her where she has her bunker.
She ran through the street.
She ran through your street and ran into her house and picked up the-- the board under the bed and crawled in with Morris.
Crawled in in the bunker and cover up.
Now--
No.
I was not there.
This-- this was before I came from the hard labor camp.
She told me that, you know.
So she went there.
And this way, she survived.
After this, three days later they called in.
We making a ghetto.
That's what they make a ghetto, after that.
But this is before I came.
Now when I came already, it was very hard.
Also food very hard.
So we had people they were smuggling in-- from other cities they were smuggling in all kind of food.
And also my wife had a rich man in the same street where she used to live, a neighbor.
A Ukrainian.
Very rich neighbor.
So he-- she smuggled out under the wires, and she went to him.
And he gave her food also.
So once with the smuggling, bringing in food and everything, so I opened up-- we open up like a restaurant in the ghetto.
I opened up, actually, a restaurant in the ghetto.
Open up.
We had a stove what I built, a stove from-- from bricks.
Build a stove to bake bread.
We were baking bread.
We were baking cake.
And smugglers used to come in, smuggle in the food, and we had special boys.
But they were bringing in food.
We had one from my city from Katowice.
He is still in Lakewood, New Jersey.
He had a butcher store till now.
So he used to smuggle in food.
And sometimes he didn't have food.
So he came in.
He say, eh, [SPEAKING POLISH].
We have to eat today anyway.
But we were given a lot of food for-- kids came in with little kettles.
[SPEAKING POLISH] You know, from--
No.
The German police didn't came to the ghetto.
Because if they need taken out people every day for work, the Jewish police did that.
Some Jewish police, maybe there was one or two, they were a little rough.
That they shouldn't be so rough.
But the most of them were very nice, you know.
We were-- it's all-- you know, it's living in the same city, you know.
So it was not so bad.
Now, those people, what they had money, they used to pay.
They used to pay us.
But giving out money for the kids, giving out food for the kids, what they have to give them.
Also they brought my whole family from Lublin.
My grandmother died.
Died or she was killed.
Or she was killed.
I really don't know because they took her bed.
Ukrainians came and they took her bed away.
My uncle used to send money.
Say, Ma, buy yourself a new bed, new furniture, and this is what's by her so-- so dear.
That's when they took her out from the bed or she died, or they took the bed and they killed her.
My aunt came to the ghetto.
My cousins came to the ghetto.
My uncles came to the ghetto.
So I saw the whole family.
Also, my mother-- this was my father's family.
Then my mother's family also came from-- from the villages, what they had a ghetto there.
So they came also here, and I came to visit them.
You know, and I came from the concentration camp.
Came to see them.
So whatever we could, I say, send the kids.
You know, mainly the kids, the cousins used to came.
We used to give them food.
Now, on April was the liquidation of the ghetto.
This was the finish.
So my wife's cousin had a ghetto-- had a-- had a bunker not far, right next to the Gentile people.
There was his house, and he build-- he had a bunker there, a cellar.
And he told a lot of his friends and family where he has a bunker.
He himself, when he heard the shots, he was by the cousin.
By his cousin, by my wife's cousin.
Oh, but this cousin what-- was the baker, what I bring him the food.
He-- he was there.
So he couldn't run to his bunker, but he went right away to their bunker.
Now, they find his bunker, the Germans.
In this bunker, cousin's bunker, we were 32 people.
When we came in, we were already the last because we were about three blocks away to run to his bunker.
And we used to be.
So I grabbed my brother was with me.
I grabbed him.
And also came with us my wife's brother-in-law, the husband's brother was with us too.
His name?
His name-- forget already his name.
So he came.
So I grabbed my mother.
My brother was also very sick, so I grab him by the hand and we run to that bunker.
Now, in the middle of-- of that, run to the bunker, he said, Leon, I can't go yet.
Very bad cramps.
He couldn't walk.
So he say, I have to go back.
I have to go to the toilet.
I can't walk.
Oh, maybe I shouldn't let him go.
I should grab him and take him with me.
But he say he's going back.
Just-- soon he came back.
The gestapo was soon right there.
And they grabbed him.
Now, the brother-in-law was still going with us running.
And the little kid, Morris.
We are run to the bunker.
We came.
We know where it is.
We went into a hole.
There was a double wall.
A double wall made.
Like here's the wall.
We make a double wall here.
And there was boards, all from boards.
We took out a board, and we all went in through a stepladder.
Went in.
So first I send my wife and the-- and Morris.
Then I was going there too, already.
And it was clear.
But in the meanwhile, we went down.
And he has to work.
The brother-in-law, my wife's brother-in-law, supposed to go down also.
He saw the-- the gestapo already in-- coming into the house close.
So he locked us up.
He let us in, and we locked out.
And we were looking inside.
Where is he?
He is not there.
He was afraid.
When he is going to go away, he's going to see that he goes in.
There was 32 people with us already in the bunker.
So they grabbed him.
The gestapo grabbed him, and they took him away, and he was killed.
Now, what's happened.
We look, and we call him by the names.
So went out of my head.
We called him.
Nobody-- he doesn't answer.
And it was not too much light there.
We had only water.
There was no food.
And then we heard already it's starting, the whole megillah.
They grabbed all around.
They took people.
Everybody who was not in bunkers.
A lot of people was in bunkers.
They grabbed everybody, took them on trucks.
Later, the Ukrainians told us what they did, because they live not close-- close.
Was right close by the forest.
They took like 100 boys, young men.
They took them and they let them make the graves.
They made the graves, deep graves.
And later, they shot them and they throw them in right in the graves.
And then they bring people, Jews, with the trucks.
Mothers and fathers and children on their hand team.
And they went out with a machine gun, fell in, and later they buried them alive.
They shot the mother.
The baby was alive.
You know.
And when we came out after two and a half days, we were afraid to go out.
And we went-- we had to go-- we went through, we saw the graves.
We saw the blood.
Everything was boiling up because people buried alive.
Mostly people were buried alive.
We came to the woods and we saw all of that.
Now, those people, they know a list how much was in ghetto.
This, they know, approximately.
So they see there's missing-- a lot of people are missing.
So they start to burn the ghetto.
They burned down the ghetto.
Soon, the smoke start going in in the bunker-- in the bunker.
So people were coming out.
People come out, and they shoot them.
They didn't take them no more.
They shoot them right on the spot, everybody.
They came-- when-- but our house was next-- by the neighbor's house.
So the neighbors said to the gestapo, if this house, you burn this house, you have to go far away.
Not to let this house burning.
Because otherwise, his whole property-- it was just the wires go like this close.
You can't burn our house.
So they didn't burn the house.
But they put-- before they burned the houses, they put a lot of stuff in our houses, clothes what the-- what the-- what the Germans took out.
You know, whatever they found, nice clothes, they put it in this house, they put it in.
So anyway, we survived.
After-- on the third day, I was going.
They was still working and cleaning out the smoke and the fire, you know.
They had a lot of Ukrainians what they-- what they-- they had took, what they should help and to clean up all of that.
On the third day, I was going out every-- at night.
Going up.
Listening.
Do I hear-- I hear noises.
Let's go.
You can't go out.
You can't go up.
On the third day right in the early--
No.
There was nothing.
There was-- at night.
No we didn't have no food at all.
Nothing at all.
We had just water.
We had just water to drink.
We wouldn't expecting-- they say it's going to be on actions.
It's not going to be an action.
Nobody know.
So we went out at night, like 12 o'clock midnight, or 11 o'clock, I think.
We went out.
I went out.
And I listen.
I hear it's nothing.
I went out, checked, quiet.
I tell them, everybody up.
Everybody came up.
They were 32 people.
A lot of people-- there was still another ghetto call them with a W.
There was-- they were working for the Wehrmacht.
A W means the Wehrmacht, the German government.
They were working, because they had the German government.
There was another ghetto where they-- where they needed-- those people had a W on their clothes that they can't go out free.
Because they were going out, they took them to work.
They took them to work.
So-- for the Germans.
So they had a W.
They had 70 people there.
A lot of people smuggled in, like my-- my father's cousin, a lady what she was with us later.
She and a boy.
And he was there also.
But he didn't have the W.
So there was more than 70 people there.
People, you know.
So what's happened-- oh, yeah.
When I went out, where do I go?
People go-- people go and smuggled into that little ghetto.
Everybody had someplace where to go or to hide later on.
But two girls, 15-- no, 11 or 14-year-old girls, they didn't have where to go.
They say, we have no parents.
Their parents got killed before.
They were with strangers.
So they want to go with me.
I say, I don't know myself.
I know where I wanted to go.
But I don't know if he's going to let me go.
But then they start crying.
Where should I let them like this?
So I took them along.
I took them along, and we went in the woods.
So we saw all of that, you know, the graves, everything with the blood.
I want to go-- I was going to that farm what he had our fields.
I was going-- that's what I was thinking.
You know, all the time.
You know, when I was in the bunker.
That's the only place I can go.
I don't have nowhere to go.
I'll go to him.
So I figure I'll go there.
Soon we came through the woods.
We were very tired.
And we start walking.
And the stars coming out.
It was cloudy in the beginning, later stars coming out.
And then came out the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what you call the [POLISH].
I forgot already in English.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
It came out.
A lot of light came out.
And there was white trees.
And my wife got so scared.
All the white trees, you know.
Got-- she got scared and she start screaming.
Leon, where you going?
This is the gestapo.
This is the gestapo.
This is the gestapo.
She got mixed up completely.
Her mind.
Thought that every tree is a gestapo.
I say, Ruthie, this is not a gestapo.
What are you talking about?
No!
This is gestapo.
And she screams.
So I sat her down, and I quiet her down.
And she thinks that this is all gestapo.
I say, this is not gestapo.
And later on, I quiet her down.
We were sitting for a while.
We were hungry.
There was no water, no nothing, till we come to a lake.
But then before we came to a lake, I look around.
Morris, his head was going like this.
Bends.
He went down on the floor, on the-- on the grass.
So I start shaking him.
He doesn't-- he doesn't react.
He was six years old.
He doesn't react.
Six or seven already.
He was already seven years old. 1936.
Yeah.
This was 1943.
Yeah. 1943.
So he collapsed.
And I see, what can I give him?
We have nothing.
So I ran out and I looked around and I found chaff, the little grass, you know.
They call chaff.
If you know about it.
I pull that chaff, that grass, and I grab a couple of bunches and I went in, I squeezed it and I gave him in his mouth.
And he start breathing up.
Start breathing up.
And I told him to chew it up, and he chew it up, and he came to himself.
Came to himself.
So I took him on my hands, and he was on my hands for a couple miles.
I got tired.
And then I let him down and he walk.
We walked him and we walked till I came-- it was about miles-- four, five miles, maybe six, maybe more.
And we came in-- in the middle of the night, we came in to that farm, to Stefan Marko.
And I knocked on the door-- in the window.
And he came to the window.
Woke him up.
He came to the window.
And he opened up.
He saw me through the window.
He opened up right away the door.
Say, Leon, what are you doing here?
I heard that they burned the ghetto down in Jaworow, and I didn't even know where you are, that you were in the ghetto, but that's what I heard.
The whole ghetto was burned down.
They are liquidate everybody.
I say, no.
I came out from the ghetto.
I'm alive.
And I got here-- this is my wife.
He never met her before.
And this is my stepson.
And this is two girls, cousins, I told him.
Cousins from my mother's side.
So he-- I say, I want to stay by you for a while till I know what we can do.
Maybe you can build-- maybe you can build a bunker for us by you, and the-- on the-- on your place and we can stay.
Now, he was a very intelligent, nice man.
Very nice man.
So we sat there for three days.
And I told him, I want to come.
I don't have no money.
I don't have nothing.
I want to come here.
I want to be prepared.
I want to go to that small ghetto.
There is still a small ghetto.
And I want to go.
Maybe I'll find some people.
So altogether, we are three.
And I'll find two more people.
So I come with money.
And that you can support us till the war will be over.
Till the Germans will be away.
He say, OK.
He-- he said yes.
Well, it's his wife came out.
And they put up a letter from the outside to in on top where the cows were.
And he gave us blankets.
He gave us there.
And all kind of stuff.
And she brought up food, quietly, and everything.
And we ate.
And then I talked over with him a lot of things what I figure what to do.
You know, and all that stuff.
So he say-- I say, OK.
I'm going to go and I'm going to come back.
This is those two little girls, I'm going to leave it.
And that's what happened.
We went back.
We smuggled into that little-- it was not easy.
It was not easy to go back to that ghetto and the whole-- and the whole thing used to go to be afraid how to come to that ghetto, how to smuggle in.
But somehow, here you have to hide.
People are going here.
You have to lay down.
You have to do this.
Oh, that ghetto.
It was just the W ghetto.
That-- that was how, from the Wehrmacht.
I mean from the German.
That was the-- that was the name.
The W ghetto.
The V.
The V Ghetto.
Not the V, the W.
The V was different.
So anyway, and we came to that ghetto.
I found my friend from before the war what he was in the hard labor camp.
And I found him.
I know him and his sister was there.
Which friend was all?
This was Beilare.
Beilare.
He was-- his father was a butcher.
Beilare.
Beilare.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Beilare.
Forgot his name, his second name.
Yeah.
And I talked to him.
I say I came so-and-so.
I was here.
And we have a hiding place.
And they-- he was-- he with other people were organized to go in the partisans, to go out.
They have rifles.
They have guns.
They were prepared, like 15 or 17 people to go in the woods and to be like partisans.
And that's what they were figuring to do, because they know that it's going to be liquidated too in a short time.
So I say, I want to find some people whatever they want to go with us.
But I can take only two people.
He said, the [POLISH].
They were very rich before the war.
They were very rich.
Three-- two brothers and a nephew.
So-- so he say, I'll take you to the [POLISH].
I say, but I cannot go to the [POLISH].
They were three people.
They were two brothers and a boy.
Say, I cannot take them.
It would have been better man than woman to take along, you know.
But this way, I can't take.
So then he say, there's another woman, a very wealthy woman.
They have coal mines in Poland.
Very wealthy.
And she was the only one.
Her brother was someplace else hiding, and she was here.
So he gave me-- he came with me and he say, this is the Scher.
Their name was also Scher, exactly the spelling.
But no family at all.
So I came out, tell her who I am, so-and-so.
And I say, I have a place where we hide, and we'll go.
She say, I'm ready.
I have a lot of money.
I have diamonds.
I have everything with everyone.
She brought out a cassette, opened up, with everything.
Gold dollar bills. 20 gold dollar bills. 20s.
I didn't count them, but I saw a lot.
Her name is Scher.
Forgot also her first name.
Yeah.
So we say, all right.
Anytime-- anytime you're ready, I'll go with you.
I say, yes.
I'm-- I'm going to be ready in a day or two, because I have to prepare my wife and child.
We have no clothes, and there's a lot of--
She say, what?
You have a wife and child?
No, no.
Forget it.
You have a wife and child.
I'm not going with you.
She was afraid of the child and-- she told she'll go with me, you know, two people will be fine.
We can live like kings, you know, with money.
With a lot of money.
So anyway, she say, no, I can't go.
She said, you want to leave your wife and child, you want to leave, then it's a different story.
I said, no, I'm sorry.
So I left.
Then I saw again Beilare.
Beilare, it doesn't work.
I have to-- somebody else.
He say there's here a lady from [POLISH].
She has got a nephew.
The nephew is a young boy 14, 15 years old.
Her nephew.
Because her husband-- she is the cousin.
She is my father's cousin.
So later I find out who she is.
So she is my father's cousin.
And the boy is her husband's, her nephew.
Her husband got killed also the first day, what I told you, when we came to the concentrate-- to hard labor camp, Jaktorow.
Because he couldn't run.
So-- so I told her about her husband.
He got killed in-- in the same concentration camp.
So anyway--
